name: Write Git Revert Recovery Section

tasks.title: Add git revert recovery section to failure-and-recovery.md
tasks.description: Add git revert recovery section to failure-and-recovery.md
tasks.body:
Add a new section to docs/humans/failure-and-recovery.md titled 'Git Revert Recovery' (or similar). The section should explain:

1. **Atomic commits**: The daemon commits code changes and .wolfcastle/ state together after every task iteration, so each commit is a complete snapshot of the project at that point.

2. **Reading the commit log**: wolfcastle commits use the format 'wolfcastle: <task-id> complete' or 'wolfcastle: <task-id> partial (attempt N)'. Users can scan git log to find the commit before a bad task outcome.

3. **Reverting**: To undo a bad task's work, revert to the commit before it using git revert or git reset. Then restart the daemon, which will re-evaluate the task from the clean state.

4. **Concrete example**: Show a realistic git log output with wolfcastle-style commit messages, identify the bad commit, and demonstrate the git revert command sequence. Keep the example grounded in the commit message format defined in the deterministic-git spec.

Cross-reference the existing 'Partial Work Preservation' and 'Self-Healing' sections. Reference the git config section. Match the existing document's voice and structure (prose-forward, conversational, technically precise).
acceptance_criteria: A 'Git Revert Recovery' section exists in failure-and-recovery.md
Section explains that daemon commits code and state atomically after every iteration
Section shows how to read wolfcastle commit messages in git log
Section includes a concrete example with git log output and git revert commands
Section explains restarting the daemon after revert to retry the task

tasks.title: Audit
tasks.description: Verify all work in Write Git Revert Recovery Section is complete and correct

audit_enrichment: Verify the example uses the correct wolfcastle commit message format from the deterministic-git spec
Verify cross-references to Partial Work Preservation and Self-Healing sections are present and correct

breadcrumbs.text: Added Git Revert Recovery section to docs/humans/failure-and-recovery.md covering atomic commits, commit log format, git revert/reset workflows, and daemon restart after revert

Audit reviewed docs/humans/failure-and-recovery.md (151 lines, 52 lines added). PASS. Build clean, all tests pass. One minor finding accepted: the multi-commit revert example narrative describes reverting two commits from the same task, but the SHAs in the example belong to two different tasks (task-0003 and task-0002). The git syntax is correct regardless, and the intent is clear to the reader. Cross-references to Partial Work Preservation, Self-Healing, and git config are all valid. Config field names (commit_on_success, commit_state) verified in codebase. Section placement between Partial Work Preservation and Self-Healing is logical. Voice matches the existing document. Quality: clean, well-structured documentation that fills a genuine gap in the recovery story.

audit.result_summary: Added a Git Revert Recovery section to docs/humans/failure-and-recovery.md documenting how users can undo bad task output using git history. The section covers atomic commit structure, commit log format conventions, single and multi-commit revert workflows using both git revert and git reset, and daemon restart after revert. Includes a realistic git log example grounded in the wolfcastle commit message format. Cross-references Partial Work Preservation, Self-Healing, and git configuration. Audit verified build, tests, config field references, and document structure.

task-0001.objective: Add a git revert recovery section to failure-and-recovery.md documenting how users can undo bad task output using git history
task-0001.what_happened: Added a new 'Git Revert Recovery' section between Partial Work Preservation and Self-Healing. The section covers atomic commit structure, reading the commit log format, reverting single and multi-commit task outputs with both git revert and git reset, and restarting the daemon after revert. Included a concrete git log example using realistic wolfcastle commit messages.
went_well: The section slots naturally between Partial Work Preservation (which it builds on) and Self-Healing (which it cross-references). The example git log output grounds the explanation in something scannable.
improvements: The git revert example for multi-commit scenarios could show the --no-commit flag for batching reverts into a single commit, but that felt like over-documenting for the audience.
